perturbation.original_text: During the Consultation Period (as defined below) and for a period of one year thereafter, except in connection with his performance of the Services, the Consultant shall not engage in any activity in the field described on Schedule A to this Agreement, and he shall not assist any other person or organization that engages in any such activity.
perturbation.changed_text: During the Consultation Period and thereafter, the Consultant shall not engage in any activity that directly competes with the Company, except as explicitly agreed upon in writing.
perturbation.explanation: The original text specifies a limited non-compete tied to 'the field described on Schedule A'. The modified text broadens this to 'any activity that directly competes', creating an ambiguity. The original Schedule A describes a specific field (drugs and cell-based therapies designed for tissue-specific immunosuppression). The revised clause removes this specificity, contradicting the implicit reliance on Schedule A for defining the scope of non-competition. This makes it difficult to enforce because the definition of 'directly competes' is not provided elsewhere.
perturbation.contradicted_text:
SCHEDULE A

Consultant will provide advice and services to the Company as requested by the Board of Directors from time to time. The field for purposes of Section 1 is defined as drugs and cell-based therapies designed for tissue-specific immunosuppression.
perturbation.justification: The original text limits the non-compete to the 'field described on Schedule A'. The changed text broadens the non-compete to 'any activity that directly competes with the Company'. Schedule A defines a very specific field ('drugs and cell-based therapies designed for tissue-specific immunosuppression'). The modified text removes this specificity and relies on the undefined term 'directly competes', creating a contradiction because the scope of the non-compete is no longer tied to the definition provided in Schedule A, making it ambiguous and potentially unenforceable. This directly conflicts with the implicit definition provided by Schedule A, which is referenced in the original clause, thus triggering an 'Omissions – In Text Contradiction'.

perturbation.original_text: Subject to approval of the Board of Directors of the Company, the Company shall issue and sell to the Consultant, and the Consultant shall purchase from the Company, subject to the terms and conditions set forth in this Agreement and the Restricted Stock Agreement, 1,990,000 shares (the "Shares") of common stock, $0.0001 par value, of the Company ("Common Stock"), at a purchase price of $0.0001 per share, for an aggregate purchase price of $190. Such Shares are in addition to the 10,000 shares of Common Stock held by the Consultant as of the date hereof. The Consultant agrees that the Shares shall be subject to the terms of the Restricted Stock Agreement.
perturbation.changed_text: Subject to approval of the Board of Directors of the Company, the Company shall issue to the Consultant shares of common stock of the Company. The Consultant agrees that the shares shall be subject to certain restrictions.
perturbation.explanation: By removing the specific number of shares (1,990,000), the par value, and purchase price, the modified text creates an in-text contradiction. Section 3.1 now vaguely refers to 'shares of common stock' without quantity or price, while Section 3.1 mentions the Restricted Stock Agreement. This creates uncertainty because the key terms of the stock grant are missing. This also makes the references in other sections where the shares being mentioned, makes other sections contradictory as well.
perturbation.contradicted_text: 3.1 Shares. In connection with the execution of this Agreement, Consultant and Company shall enter into a Restricted Stock Agreement. Subject to approval of the Board of Directors of the Company, the Company shall issue to the Consultant shares of common stock of the Company. The Consultant agrees that the shares shall be subject to certain restrictions.
perturbation.justification: The changed text omits essential details regarding the number of shares, par value, and purchase price, creating a contradiction with the original text and the contradicted text. The contradicted text, which also appears to be present in the changed text in a modified form, now vaguely refers to 'shares of common stock' without specifying quantity or price, conflicting with the implicit requirements of the original text which outlined these details. This omission introduces ambiguity and uncertainty regarding the terms of the stock grant, violating the 'Omissions – In Text Contradiction' definition.

perturbation.original_text: For purposes of this Section 4, "Cause" shall exist in the event of (i) a good faith finding by the Board of Directors of the Company (A) of repeated and willful failure of the Consultant after written notice to perform his reasonable Services for the Company, or (B) that the Consultant has engaged in dishonesty, gross negligence or misconduct; or (ii) the conviction of the Consultant of, or the entry of a pleading of guilty or nolo contendere by the Consultant to, any crime involving moral turpitude or any felony.
perturbation.changed_text: For purposes of this Section 4, "Cause" shall exist in the event of a material breach of this agreement.
perturbation.explanation: By removing the specific definition of "Cause," including the conditions related to the consultant's behavior or conviction of a crime, the modified text becomes internally contradictory. Section 4 still references 'Cause' as a reason for termination, but the definition is simplified to 'material breach of this agreement'. This creates ambiguity because 'material breach' is a broad term that doesn't encompass the specific scenarios previously listed. The original intention behind cause is removed, leading to uncertainty in enforcement. The original cause included gross negligence and repeated failure. Now it only includes material breach which is more vauge.
perturbation.contradicted_text: 4. Termination. The Company may terminate the Consultation Period at any time for Cause (as defined below). In the event of such termination, the Consultant shall be entitled to payment hereunder and for expenses paid or incurred prior to the effective date of termination. Such payments shall constitute full settlement of any and all claims of the Consultant of every description against the Company. Notwithstanding the foregoing, the Company may terminate the Consultation Period effective immediately upon receipt of written notice by the Consultant, if the Consultant breaches or threatens to breach any provision of Section 6. For purposes of this Section 4, "Cause" shall exist in the event of a material breach of this agreement.
perturbation.justification: The changed text replaces a specific definition of 'Cause' with a much broader one ('material breach of this agreement'). The contradicted text (which is the same text, now modified) refers to 'Cause' as grounds for termination. The original definition included specific instances of misconduct or failure to perform services. By removing those specific instances, the application of 'Cause' becomes uncertain and potentially unenforceable in situations that would have previously constituted 'Cause.' This creates a direct contradiction and ambiguity because the term now lacks the specific boundaries it once had, rendering it inconsistent with its original intention. The modified definition, thus, creates a strong in-text contradiction as it is not backward compatible with the original, more specific, meaning. This is a clear case of Omissions - In Text Contradiction.
